title: Ex-athlete 'taken in' … again
subtitle: Read the text below and decide which answer best fits each gap. Choose one variant only for questions 1–8.
passage:
The home of [[0]] athlete Helen Barnett was burgled this weekend and a large number of sporting medals and trophies were [[1]]. Ms Barnett, who now [[2]] a successful sportswear company, is [[3]] to be 'devastated' at the loss.

The burglary took [[4]] on Saturday afternoon when Ms Barnett went to investigate smoke coming from a wooded area in her large two-acre garden. The burglar is thought to have [[5]] fire to undergrowth in order to attract Barnett out of the house. A young man carrying a large bag was seen climbing over the garden wall, before making his [[6]] in a sports car.

The theft comes just ten months after a similar incident in which a man posing as a telephone engineer had [[7]] the ex-athlete into leaving the house while another helped himself to her jewels. The thieves were eventually caught and [[8]] to four years in jail.

items.explainCorrect: Passive theft of property: medals 'were stolen'. Robbed/mugged usually take a person as object; 'lifted' is informal for shoplifting.

explainIfChosen.0: Robbed normally takes a person as object (you rob someone), not trophies as subject.
explainIfChosen.1: You mug a person in the street — not sporting medals.
explainIfChosen.2: 'Lifted' is slang for stealing small items — weak with trophies and formal news style.

items.explainCorrect: She manages the firm day to day — collocate 'run a company'.

explainIfChosen.1: 'Overtakes' is passing another vehicle or competitor — wrong domain.
explainIfChosen.2: 'Works a company' is not idiomatic; we say works for/at a company.
explainIfChosen.3: 'Holds a company' does not mean manage.

items.explainCorrect: Reporting phrase: 'is said to be devastated' (journalistic passive).

explainIfChosen.0: 'Is spoken to be' is not English.
explainIfChosen.2: 'Is felt to be' is grammatically possible but odd for reported rumour here.
explainIfChosen.3: 'Is told to be' places wrong emphasis — people aren't ordering her emotional state.

items.explainCorrect: Fixed phrase: an event 'takes place' = happens.

explainIfChosen.0: 'Take part' needs 'in' — not 'the burglary took part'.
explainIfChosen.1: 'Take hold' exists but does not fit timing of the crime.

items.explainCorrect: 'Set fire to' is the standard collocation for deliberately starting a blaze.

explainIfChosen.0: 'Made fire' is not the phrase — we don't collocate 'make fire to'.
explainIfChosen.3: 'Put fire to' is not idiomatic here.

items.explainCorrect: 'Make one's getaway' = escape after a crime.

explainIfChosen.1: A runaway is a person who has fled — not the noun after 'making his'.
explainIfChosen.2: A hideaway is a secret retreat — not quick escape by car.
explainIfChosen.3: Takeaway is food or a shop — absurd here.

explainIfChosen.0: 'Succeeded the ex-athlete into leaving' is ungrammatical (succeed + to-V / in -ing).
People are imprisoned — but standard headline/legal passive with length is 'sentenced to X years'.
explainIfChosen.1: Judges don't 'order' someone 'to four years' in this pattern.
explainIfChosen.2: 'Given to four years' is not English.
